data.title: Sibanye Stillwater: Deep Value And A Major EPS Rebound Coming (Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

This is my second time covering Sibanye Stillwater Limited (SBSW), a global company that produces large amounts of Platinum Group Metals, or PGMs, and is also expanding into battery minerals such as lithium. Sibanye operates mines and processing plants in South Africa, the United States, and Europe, and it's also involved in the cleaner energy sector.

In one year, it's had an incredible tech-like run, doubling in share value at one point since my Buy rating. Looking ahead to Sibanye’s earnings in a few days, I'm maintaining my bullish stance because I think there’s a reasonable case for continued optimism. Pricing has firmed up. And if management really stuck to the plan on costs and execution, then that discipline should show up in the numbers.

Sibanye Stillwater: Strategic Expansion and Market Leverage

Sibanye Stillwater is expected to share its full-year 2025 results on February 20. Analysts are modeling 10.37B in 2026. The core driver, as you know, is higher realized prices for PGMs and gold. There's a wide range of reasons prices have been rising, but let's not veer too far off into arguing things, like the destruction of wealth via fiat currencies, and just pinpoint it to the mainstream reasons: limited supplies and global political tensions. So as long as those constraints stick around, pricing should stay supportive.

I’m also confident, especially because of the U.S. PGM business. Last week, the U.S. Department of Commerce made a preliminary call that Russian palladium was being dumped into the U.S. They calculated a 132.83% dumping margin. If this plan actually goes through, the government could start charging import taxes at the same rate as soon as next week.

This is a big deal for the Stillwater mines in Montana. They’re basically the only meaningful U.S. PGM producer that isn’t tied to Russia. For years, subsidized metal flooded the market and crushed prices. Last year, Stillwater had to cut production in half, down to roughly 300,000 ounces. That hurt. But now, as you are well aware, prices are acting significantly better. Palladium has stabilized off the lows. And platinum has gone ballistic.

Last month, CEO Stewart said that even though near-term volatility is still there, the industry has now reached a higher minimum price level. That lines up with what Sibanye indicated last month. Stewart walked through their plan to squeeze more out of what they already have. Specifically, they’re focusing on higher-grade ore at Stillwater East and East Boulder. By concentrating on these higher-quality areas, the company may be able to become profitable again without needing to restart operations immediately at Stillwater West.

The antidumping ruling strengthens Sibanye’s position when negotiating with automakers too, who depend on PGMs to make catalytic converters. And because automakers still need these metals, Sibanye may have more leverage in pricing discussions. So if prices remain stable, this could help the company generate more predictable cash flow and potentially increase its annual EBITDA significantly.

Over in South Africa, it's no surprise that gold continues to be the company’s main profit driver. The Driefontein and Kloof mines, both of which have been operating far longer than originally planned, generated R3.6 billion in operating profit the last time I covered it. Now, Sibanye is shifting its focus to newer, more efficient operations. This also includes the Burnstone project. It’s a shallower, more mechanized mine, so it should be simpler to run. The plan is to produce around 130,000 ounces of gold a year.

At the same time, the company owns a stake in Durban Roodepoort Deep Gold (DRD). Through that, they reprocess old mine waste and pull gold out of tailings, which is basically leftover mining material. Taken together, these should give the gold division a pretty long runway, something around 40 to 50 years. But don’t take my word for it. Stewart said it’s

“one of our most successful investments, both from a production and a financial perspective… I think we invested about ZAR 2 billion to get our stake in DRD. Today, it's worth ZAR 27 billion to us.”

In South Africa, production is expected to remain steady at 1.5 million ounces by extending the life of existing operations (known as brownfield projects), including K4 and Siphumelele. These extensions require R4.4 billion in investment and are expected to keep production stable for the next 10 years. Meanwhile, management is targeting about 2.5% revenue growth. At the same time, they want to pull out $3 billion in costs. The way they plan to do that is with a hybrid operating model. In other words, big growth calls get centralized, but site teams still run day-to-day operations.

Between its operations in Montana and South Africa, Sibanye Stillwater has built a stronger presence in well-established mining regions. However, the company’s growth plans go far beyond these traditional mining locations. Europe’s Keliber lithium project in Finland shows how Sibanye Stillwater is planning ahead in the battery metals market. The project began phased commissioning in early 2026. The first phase focuses on mining and processing the ore to produce spodumene concentrate, which can already be sold profitably at current market prices. If lithium market conditions improve, the company has the option to move further and process the material into technical-grade lithium hydroxide or even battery-grade lithium hydroxide, depending on how strong the market recovery is.

The Keliber lithium project has been officially recognized as a strategic project by the European Union. It has a fully integrated setup, meaning lithium is mined and refined within the same project, something that's unique in Europe. This is especially important because more than 70% of global lithium processing currently takes place in China, something that I just discussed in more detail here. Once the project reaches full production capacity, it could produce enough lithium hydroxide to supply batteries for up to 3 million electric vehicles per year.

Just as important, the company is working to lock in cheaper, more reliable power. This dovetails nicely with Sibanye Stillwater’s broader commitment to renewable energy. On February 6, the company signed an agreement to secure 220 MW (megawatts) of solar and wind power. Then, on February 13, it entered into a renewable energy off-take agreement with NOA Group. Together, these steps are expected to move 30% of the company’s operations onto renewable energy by the end of the year.

SBSW: Deep Value with Upside Potential

SBSW’s cash flow performance does seem to suggest a very strong opportunity for investors right now. Historically, the company’s operating cash flow yield reached 13.43%. Right now, its P/OCF (blended price-to-operating-cash-flow) ratio is 7.45x. Roughly half of the estimated fair value ratio of 15.00x.

FAST Graphs is targeting a 42.34% estimated annual return via a projected 7.38% growth rate, which does a decent job of reflecting the company’s shift into lithium production through the Keliber project and stable PGM prices.

Looking at another way to value the company, its forward EV/EBITDA ratio is 5.71x. The average for the sector is 10.00x. That means the company is trading at about a 42.93% discount compared to similar companies, another nudge that you're getting a better deal than some of its peers.

Analysts’ consensus estimates support the outlook as well. They expect EPS to grow by 330.99% by 2026, alongside revenue increasing to 10.37B (from 7.79B last year).

Dilution isn't exactly immediate, but it's not impossible, and that high LT debt at 48.04% doesn't help. All in all, competition in the gold industry remains very strong, and any operational problems in South Africa could shorten what's expected to be a long production lifespan.

Final Verdict

I think that Sibanye Stillwater warrants a Strong Buy rating upgrade. The risk–reward balance looks tilted toward potentially strong gains moving forward. Revenue growth could lead to a big jump in EPS, and the company’s cash flow appears undervalued. Growth-seeking investors with commodity tolerance should buy; risk-averse: avoid.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Disc Medicine After The CRL: Risk Now Rests On APOLLO
data.description:
Thesis

As you know by now from the 21% stock slide, the FDA has issued a CRL to Disc Medicine Inc. (IRON) for bitopertin. However, on the surface, it appears to be more of a regulatory disagreement and not about whether the drug actually lowers its biochemical target. So it seems more about whether that biomarker itself reliably predicts real clinical benefit. The issue was in the subjectivity of whether patients showed a real lifestyle benefit. As I will explain, what it came down to was that different patients experienced or even started with different levels of sunlight exposure they were comfortable with. The result is that the FDA has deemed the AURORA and BEACON not sufficient and has indicated the need to see the results of the ongoing Phase 3 APOLLO study. Conveniently, in this press release, management has told us the APOLLO enrollment and progress are several months ahead of schedule. So we can expect to see results sometime before the end of the year.

So in my opinion, what this comes down to is the new endpoints included in the APOLLO trial and if they can convince the FDA that bitopertin leads to an actual lifestyle benefit for patients. I still think this is pretty difficult to do after going over the designs of the trials, as I'll explain. This is primarily the reason for going with a "Hold" on the stock. There was a lot of investor confidence baked into the price, with shares up about 32% on the 1-year chart before the sell-off yesterday. If the second time around also leads to a similar response from the FAD, in that there is just no direct way to statistically measure patient benefit due to the subjectivity, then there may be more downside for the stock. If investors are choosing to buy into the stock on this current dip, I would advise them to get to know the specific details of the APOLLO trial and understand the risk in the new endpoint.

Background

First, just for a bit of background, bitopertin is an oral inhibitor of glycine transporter 1 (GlyT1). GlyT1 is a membrane transporter we often see on developing red blood cells whose aim is to supply glycine for heme biosynthesis. You see, in erythropoietic protoporphyria (EPP), defective ferrochelatase activity is what leads to the accumulation of metal-free protoporphyrin IX (PPIX), which is a small phototoxic molecule that causes severe pain and tissue injury upon light exposure. So by limiting glycine uptake itself, bitopertin, in theory, should reduce the substrate supply for heme synthesis further upstream. Thus, this should decrease the production of PPIX. Mechanistically speaking, the approach is a bit complicated but would be classed as disease-modifying rather than symptomatic. It doesn’t just try to block the pain/inflammation, but instead it targets the biochemical pathway responsible for toxic PPIX buildup in erythroid cells. So, in simpler terms, it's best understood as bitopertin basically slows down heme production in our red blood cells by blocking glycine uptake. This is key, and as a result, this later reduces the formation of toxic protoporphyrin (PPIX) that causes light-induced pain in EPP.

FDA Complete Response Letter for Bitopertin

So overall, NDA initially relied primarily on data from the Phase 2 AURORA, a randomized and placebo-controlled trial. In addition, the BEACON open-label trials showed statistically significant reductions in whole-blood metal-free PPIX, which was the proposed surrogate endpoint for accelerated approval. Now, the FDA agreed on the first requirement for accelerated approval, which is that bitopertin clearly lowers PPIX levels. However, the accelerated approval also requires that the surrogate endpoint be reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit. This is what caught Disc Medicine out. This is what the FDA concluded that the trials did not demonstrate: a meaningful association between the percent reduction in PPIX and the improvement in sunlight-exposure-based endpoints. In terms of the exposure to sunlight, they used things like measured amounts of time in sunlight without pain or phototoxic reactions.

So, in other words, although the biomarker moved in the right direction and, in general, the biological rationale was strong, it was the clinical outcome measures used in both AURORA and BEACON that did not convincingly track with the biomarker changes. It's a bit of a disconnect and one that likely reflects challenges inherent to EPP trials. You will always have that high variability in sunlight exposure behavior with very subjective pain endpoints. The limited trial duration also didn't help.

You see, in patients with EPP, their symptoms depend pretty heavily on how much sunlight they choose, or I guess more accurately, how much they’re able to expose themselves to. That’s not something a trial can truly control. For example, two patients with the same PPIX reduction might in reality behave very differently. One might cautiously avoid the sun because years of conditioning and fear of pain have told them to do so. On the other hand, another patient may test their limits much more aggressively. So that means the time in sunlight or other endpoints ends up not being driven just by the drug effect, but also by psychology and lifestyle. That’s what the FDA didn’t like: the fact that there's this noise in the data about whether patients actually did better in real life. In my opinion, it’s going to be hard to show a tight correlation between that biochemical change in lower PPIX and a behavioral/experiential outcome of more sun tolerance for Disc Medicine.

Cause for Investor Concern

Another area I see cause for concern is in pain and phototoxic reactions. These are both subjective and episodic, so not really continuous measurements. Some patients may experience severe pain after just a small bit of exposure, and others just mild discomfort after longer exposure. Also, recall bias affects diary-based reporting here. When trials are quite short, we usually see them fail to capture enough real-world sunlight events to actually robustly demonstrate benefit. So even if bitopertin is doing what it is supposed to be doing biologically, which is lowering PPIX in red blood cells, the problem is that the trial endpoints may not move in parallel in a statistically convincing way. From the FDA’s perspective here, it introduces a lot of uncertainty about whether the biomarker they chose truly predicts meaningful clinical benefit, even when the mechanistic rationale behind the candidate is strong.

So the result of all this is that the FDA issued a CRL and has directed Disc to wait for results from the ongoing Phase 3 APOLLO trial. This trial is designed to directly link the reduction in PPIX we’ve seen to clinically meaningful improvements in light tolerance and symptoms. The FDA also indicated that positive APOLLO results could potentially support traditional approval. So that shifts the program from a surrogate-driven pathway to one more or less based on confirmatory clinical benefit.

Phase 3 APOLLO Trial

So that means the narrative now shifts towards the Phase 3 APOLLO trial for bitopertin. As you can imagine, it represents the most definitive test yet of the drug’s ability to actually improve meaningful outcomes in patients with EPP. However, I know what you’re thinking: Will this trial face the same endpoint problems as the earlier trials?

Well, the APOLLO trial is actually a substantial step up from the earlier Phase 2 programs. It’s a bit different from the smaller AURORA and BEACON studies, which were mainly focused on the surrogate biochemical endpoint of PPIX reduction and then looked to later collected sunlight exposure data with limited statistical power. APOLLO was actually designed from the outset with consultation from the FDA to address the past shortcomings. It’s a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study, this time enrolling about 150 patients aged 12 and older. The patients have EPP, including X-linked protoporphyria (XLP), and are administered a single 60 mg daily dose of bitopertin over a full 6-month treatment period. Firstly, this is a much longer-duration design. So that's an improvement and should help the chance of detecting true differences in clinical outcomes.

Most importantly, however, APOLLO features co-primary endpoints. So we have the average monthly total time in sunlight without pain during the last month of treatment, which is a direct, patient-centric measure of benefit that reflects real quality-of-life improvement. We also have the percent change in whole blood metal-free PPIX after 6 months, which should allow the trial to simultaneously confirm both the clinical benefit and the biological effect. These endpoints have been agreed upon with the FDA to make the trial all-around more statistically robust and to better address regulatory expectations about linking PPIX reduction to decent lifestyle outcomes.

So the key here is that APOLLO directly measures pain-free sunlight exposure. This is the big improvement over earlier trials, where subjective reports and variable sunlight exposure limited the FDA’s interpretability. Enrollment is ongoing but set to be completed soon in March 2026. Management mentioned strong patient and physician interest and topline data to be expected sometime in 4Q26. The company also mentioned they have plans to use those results to support a traditional approval pathway and respond to CRL with an updated regulatory decision expected around mid-2027.

Valuation

From a valuation perspective, one thing the selloff did do was make shares look a bit more attractive at current levels. Disc Medicine, however, still remains pretty difficult to assess using traditional earnings-based metrics since the company is still pre-revenue and loss-making. They have no meaningful price-to-earnings ratio. Instead, I feel the most relevant framework is balance sheet strength and enterprise value. In that sense, they hold a market cap of about $2.1 billion and an enterprise value of about $1.5 billion. This is reflective of the company holding a hefty cash position relative to its size. Disc Medicine has about $616 million in cash and only about $31 million in debt. So it would seem that they are exceptionally well capitalized for a clinical-stage biotech, which should give them a runway into 2029 and plenty of ability to complete the APOLLO Phase 3 trial. I don't see much near-term financing risk moving forward. We also saw a $200 million debt financing back in October.

Elsewhere, on relative valuation metrics, the stock trades around 3.4x price to book, which is slightly above the healthcare sector median but also pretty reasonable for a company whose primary assets are just cash and late-stage clinical programs. I also think the sharp stock drop post the CRL reduced the near-term approval probability rather than any solvency or development risk. In practical terms, the market is now valuing Disc for the most part as a quite well-funded Phase 3 company with a single key asset, but with caution around the soon-to-come confirmatory data. It leaves valuation highly sensitive to APOLLO outcomes, as we know. The bull case here would see the re-rating of the stock toward a commercial-stage multiple. On the flip side, the failure would force the market to view the company primarily as a cash-rich R&D platform with a very uncertain pipeline value.

Looking Ahead

So, going forward, look out for the expected enrollment completion of the APOLLO study sometime next month, but mainly the results of the trial, which should come in 4Q26. We need to take another look at the stock, then, specifically, the lifestyle benefits of those exposed to the sun post-treatment. If there is any kind of subjectivity or cause for variability in the lifestyle improvement data, then expect investor concern to show up in the stock price again.

data.title: ATN International: Deal Approval Risks Persist, But The Risk-Reward Still Favors Bulls
data.description:
ATN International, Inc. (ATNI) is a telecommunications and broadband infrastructure provider that serves rural and remote parts of the US and select Caribbean markets. The company owns and operates network assets, such as fiber, transport, subsea connectivity, and towers. Recently, ATNI also announced a tower portfolio divestiture with proceeds to be used as a deleveraging catalyst. And, I believe that this methodical strategy will continue to accumulate shareholder value in the long run. Given that the stock’s valuation still seems attractive, I reiterate my bullish stance on ATNI at these levels.

Deleveraging Telecom

ATN International, Inc. is a digital infrastructure and communications services operator. Concretely, their infrastructure is mostly focused on serving rural and remote areas within the US as well as parts of the Caribbean. ATNI was founded back in 1987 and is currently headquartered in Beverly, Massachusetts. I previously covered ATNI last October with a “Buy” rating, and since then the stock has risen by roughly 87.1% (excluding dividends). So, I thought it was worthwhile to write an update to my thesis.

As a quick recap, ATNI’s US businesses include an Alaska component with fiber and subsea connectivity across the state. ATNI also operates in the Caribbean with integrated telecom in select islands, plus business services. ATNI invests in networks, building or buying telecom infrastructure, becoming one of the primary providers in a territory.

After that, ATNI monetizes the network, growing the broadband subscribers and selling network capacity to other companies. Those B2B clients tend to be mobile carriers that need roaming coverage in rural areas or enterprise connectivity in certain markets. Overall, they prioritize investments where expected returns are attractive for building and upgrading telecom infrastructure like fiber, towers, subsea cables, and electronics.

Tower Sale: Deleveraging Catalyst

Having said that, their recent Q3 2025 reported once again corroborates that their strategy seems to be working. Revenues reached $183.2 million (+3% YoY) with operating income of $9.8 million (which does show their margins aren’t as great, unfortunately). Still, Cash from operations came in at $97.7 million for the first nine months of 2025, which is again slightly higher than the first nine months of 2024 with $97.4 million in CFOs. And, while this is essentially flat in terms of cash flows, my main concern is whether or not they can continue to meet interest payments. After all, the original risk still remains because leverage and interest expense remain meaningful.

Fortunately, their nine-month interest expense was only $36.4 million, which seems largely covered by their cash flows from operations. Also, during that same period, ATNI deployed approximately $60.9 million in CAPEX. So, at its core, ATNI remains a fully self-sustainable business, which largely derisks their investment thesis in my view. But, on top of that, by February 12, 2026, ATNI’s subsidiary called “Commnet” (plus some of Commnet’s own subsidiaries) agreed to sell a portion of their telecom infrastructure. Specifically, the deal included 214 towers in the US Southwest to an Everest Infrastructure Partners affiliate for up to $297 million in cash. Additionally, this deal sold their tower portfolio, with physical tower sites, ground leases, permits, site-level contracts, and the operating rights related to the towers.

Everest thinks the tower’s acquisition can increase revenue by adding tenants over time, and for ATNI, it makes sense as it can help them delever their balance sheet. And, in that sense, it’s worth noting they’ve made more progress on that front as well. ATNI’s total debt is now around $579.6 million, with a net debt ratio of 2.47x in Q3 2025, which is an improvement relative to 2.58x at the end of Q2 2025. So we can indeed see a direct relation between this recent tower sale announcement and their ongoing debt reduction. Additionally, some of those proceeds will be used for investment in existing operations and selective growth. ATNI expects an initial closing of the transaction in Q2 2026, with gross proceeds between $250 million and $270 million.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Naturally, selling assets will have an impact on ATNI’s topline. The deal’s press release mentioned that, in exchange for more cash and better leverage, removing their tower portfolio will reduce revenues by around $5 million to $7 million. Similarly, operating income will decrease by roughly $4 million to $6 million. But, ATNI intends to use approximately $70 million of the deal’s initial proceeds to repay borrowings under the CoBank revolving credit facility. In my view, this is a high-value deleveraging target because revolvers like these are often tied to floating rates and can be relatively expensive. So, paying down this particular type of liability should have an exceedingly positive impact on their cost of capital in the long run as well.

In my opinion, this deal will be a major tailwind in 2026 and beyond. And this is probably why the market has become increasingly bullish on ATNI. However, the reality is that some risks remain in this transaction. Technically speaking, the deal will be finalized only after all relevant third parties consent. After all, many towers sit on leased land, and the lease may require the landlord’s consent to assign the lease to a new owner.

So, in theory, tower lease agreements could restrict assignment or require notice to tenants for changes in operations. And, if a meaningful number of consents are delayed or denied, the deal’s closing could be delayed significantly, or even blocked, which I would imagine would play badly with the stock price by extension. Additionally, remember that there’s another condition that must be fulfilled before the deal closes. The Hart-Scott-Rodino (HSR) waiting period expiration refers to the US antitrust pre-merger review process. The waiting period is commonly 30 days for the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and the Department of Justice (DOJ) to review the notifications and approve/reject them.

Alternatively, regulators could also issue a second request, which would probably require a deeper investigation and considerably delay the transaction. But, despite those regulatory risks, I believe that a tower portfolio sale of this size isn’t likely to trigger a serious antitrust block. This type of concern usually plays a larger role in telecom mega-mergers. And, by comparison, ATNI’s current market cap is only around $430.7 million, so I doubt regulators see a major antitrust risk with this type of transaction. Lastly, I also like that ATNI has continued paying its quarterly dividends consistently. The most recent one was for $0.275 per share, which shows they can pursue their balance sheet deleveraging while also returning some cash to investors.

Lastly, I still think ATNI trades at slightly cheap valuation multiples despite its price appreciation since I last covered it. Their 10-Q from Q3 2025 shows its balance sheet had $106.6 million in cash and short-term investments. And, as previously mentioned, their total debt is now around $579.6 million. So, that way I estimate ATNI’s EV at roughly $903.7 million. At the same time, according to Seeking Alpha’s dashboard on ATNI, the company is projected to generate approximately $739.4 million in revenues this year. So that implies a 1.2 EV/S multiple, which is still very compelling compared to its sector’s median EV/S of 1.9.

Conclusion: Still Bullish Long-Term

Overall, I think that ATNI’s investment thesis remains intact. Of course, the upside potential is now more limited than when I first covered it. However, in the long run, I believe management’s deleveraging strategy and consistent dividends will continue to accumulate shareholder value for investors. Plus, at its current valuation multiples, the stock actually appears slightly undervalued relative to its peers. So, on balance, I feel it’s still justified to be bullish on ATNI at these levels. Just remember that it does have latent headline risks until its deal is finalized and regulators approve it, which is why I stick with only a “Buy” rating for now.

data.title: Edison International: Earnings Strength Amid Wildfire Overhangs (Upgrade)
data.description:
Utilities have taken flight in recent weeks. The catalyst? Sharply lower Treasury yields. The comparative interest rate trade now favors popular income plays in the equity space. In fact, on the year-on-year view, the Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU) is now outperforming the S&P 500 ETF (SPY).

Within Utilities, one name has risen big just lately. Edison International (EIX) jumped from $61 to $71 in short order. That came after a bottoming/consolidation stretch from early 2025 to the beginning of this year. I was "Neutral" on EIX back in December 2022. The stock has been a wild ride since then but sports a total return of 27%--much of that coming just this month.

Today, I’m upgrading the stock to a "Buy." The technical breakout looks real, while the valuation is quite attractive. Edison International reports Q4 results after the close on Wednesday.

EIX Outperforming XLU, SPY YoY

Back in October, EIX reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q3 non-GAAP EPS of $2.34 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $2.18, while revenue of $5.75 billion, up 11% from the same period last year, was about in line with estimates. The company narrowed its 2025 core EPS guidance to $5.95-$6.20, versus the consensus expectation of $6.03%.

Shares traded just fractionally lower in the session that followed. The 1.2% dip was well within the 4.3% options straddle pricing. Looking ahead to this week’s report, the options market prices in a small 2.9% options-implied stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the Wednesday evening report. Shares of the $27.5 billion market cap Utilities sector firm next go ex-dividend on April 7, while implied volatility is low at 28.5%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, EIX delivered solid top- and bottom-line numbers, with core EPS gains driven primarily by Southern California Edison’s (SCE) revenue uplift from the 2025 general rate case final decision. The ruling approved roughly 91% of requested capex and raised base revenue to fund critical grid investments while keeping system average rates the lowest among major California investor-owned utilities.

Q3 Highlights

The management team confirmed a tighter EPS guidance range, now $5.95–$6.20, with reaffirmed 5%–7% core EPS CAGR through 2028 off a $5.84 baseline. Helping the growth angle is a refreshed four‑year capital plan of $28–$29 billion and a projected 7%–8% rate-base CAGR, with no need for new equity under the consolidated financing plan. The top line is seen increasing by just 1%-3% annually, but there were positive signs regarding overall load growth. On Dec. 11, Edison also hiked its quarterly dividend by 6% to $0.8775.

Now, EIX was downgraded by a pair of sell-side firms lately. Wells cut the stock to Underweight with a $59 target, citing the Eaton Fire liability overhang. The stock has only skyrocketed since. Then, UBS turned less upbeat, but that was merely on valuation.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to be strong this year, with potentially more than $6 of non-GAAP per-share earnings. Out-year growth is less impressive, but EIX could achieve $6 in operating EPS by FY 28. On net, the sell-side has turned more upbeat, with nine upgrades compared to just a lone downgrade, per Seeking Alpha. Ranked No. 8 out of 115 in its sector on the Factor Grades metrics, profitability trends are very strong.

EIX: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, if we assume $6.15 of normalized non-GAAP EPS and apply its long-term average P/E of 13.33x, then shares should trade near $83. That makes this out-west utility attractive on valuation. Risks remain, though. Even if we apply a 10% discount to account for wildfire risk, the stock is still reasonably compelling on earnings.

EIX: Modest Valuation Multiples

Key risks for EIX include uncertainty regarding the wildfire compensation program—it's unclear what the average claim and the full-loss estimate will be. What’s more, there are unknowns about the management team’s plan to mitigate wildfire risk going forward. It’s also possible that natural disaster risk could result in credit downgrades in the months ahead.

Of course, higher Treasury yields could result in lower overall valuations in the Utilities sector.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q4 2025 earnings date of Wednesday, Feb. 18 AMC. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares still decently cheap amid ongoing wildfire uncertainty in California, EIX’s technical situation is strong. Notice in the chart below that shares have surged from the low $60s to the low $70s on a volume uptick. Furthermore, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph confirms the price breakout. But where might the stock climb to from here (and after the Q4 earnings report)?

Well, take a look at the wildfire-related gap from 13 months ago. That level is $77. A gap fill would absolutely make sense, so we could see a pause there. Above that, the previous highs near $88 could be a long-term target. On the downside, the rising 200-day moving average is way down at $56, so shares are a bit extended. The former uptrend resistance line is at $64, so I see that as support.

EIX: Massive Upside Move, $78 Resistance

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on Edison International. The embattled California-based Electric Utilities company has a solid EPS growth outlook, while the valuation and technicals augur for further upside with the Q4 report on tap.
